title: Multi-Class Confusion Matrix
description:
Given true labels $y_{\text{true}} \in \{0,\dots,n_c-1\}^n$, predicted labels $y_{\text{pred}} \in \{0,\dots,n_c-1\}^n$, and the number of classes $n_c$, build the $n_c \times n_c$ confusion matrix $M$ where
$$M_{r,c} = \#\{ i : y_{\text{true},i} = r \text{ and } y_{\text{pred},i} = c \}$$

so rows index the true class and columns index the predicted class.

Implement `confusion_matrix(y_true, y_pred, n_classes)` returning $M$ as an $n_c \times n_c$ integer array.

**Shapes**: `y_true`: (20,) int in $\{0,1,2\}$, `y_pred`: (20,) int in $\{0,1,2\}$, `n_classes`: 3 (scalar) -> returns $M$: (3, 3).
starter_code:
import numpy as np

def confusion_matrix(y_true, y_pred, n_classes):
    # TODO: return an n_classes x n_classes array M where M[r, c] counts
    # examples with true label r and predicted label c
    pass
reference:
import numpy as np
def confusion_matrix(y_true, y_pred, n_classes):
    y_true = np.asarray(y_true).astype(np.int64)
    y_pred = np.asarray(y_pred).astype(np.int64)
    M = np.zeros((n_classes, n_classes), dtype=np.int64)
    for t, p in zip(y_true, y_pred):
        M[t, p] += 1
    return M
hints: Initialize an n_classes x n_classes array of zeros, then loop over paired (true, pred) labels and increment M[true, pred].
This can also be vectorized with np.add.at(M, (y_true, y_pred), 1) instead of a Python loop.

solutions.name: Count pairs into a matrix
solutions.explanation: Each example contributes exactly one count to the cell indexed by its (true, predicted) label pair; accumulating these counts over all examples gives the full confusion matrix.
solutions.code:
import numpy as np
def confusion_matrix(y_true, y_pred, n_classes):
    y_true = np.asarray(y_true).astype(np.int64)
    y_pred = np.asarray(y_pred).astype(np.int64)
    M = np.zeros((n_classes, n_classes), dtype=np.int64)
    np.add.at(M, (y_true, y_pred), 1)
    return M
